era_context: Late Bronze Age, mid-second millennium BCE. Thutmose I is in the early years of his reign in Egypt. The Hittite Old Kingdom continues its consolidation. Mycenaean Greece is developing palace centers. The Shang Dynasty is in the reign of a mid-period king. This is a period of increasing inter-regional trade and diplomatic contact, though major empires have not yet fully crystallized.

events.title: Thutmose I's early reign consolidation
events.description: Thutmose I, having recently ascended to the throne (conventional dating ~1504 BCE), consolidates power within Egypt and begins preparations for military campaigns. He establishes his authority over the priesthood of Amun and the military establishment. This period sees the strengthening of the New Kingdom administrative apparatus.

sources.name: Egyptian king lists and historiographic consensus

events.certainty_note: Thutmose I's reign is well-attested, but the precise year of his accession varies by ±2–3 years depending on chronological system.

disconfirming_evidence: No specific events for -1506 are well-attested in contemporary sources. The assignment of events to this year is largely conventional, based on regnal chronologies that themselves carry significant uncertainty. Modern scholarship increasingly questions the precision of Egyptian chronology for this period.
historiographic_note: For this year, documentation is extremely sparse. No specific events are recorded in surviving sources for -1506 specifically. The year is reconstructed primarily through regnal chronologies and the assumption of continuous rule. The historical record for this period is heavily skewed toward Egypt and the Hittite heartland; vast regions of the world are entirely absent from written records.

graph_edges.note: Thutmose I's consolidation occurs during the same period as his early military campaigns
